article.title: Reye's Syndrome and Medication Use
article.authors: Halpin TJ, Holtzhauer FJ, Campbell RJ, Hall LJ, Correa-Villaseñor A, Lanese R, Rice J, Hurwitz ES

study_type.notes: Matched case-control study. 97 Reye's syndrome cases in Ohio children (onset December 1978-March 1980) matched with 156 control subjects for age, race, sex, geographic location, time, and type of illness. Classic epidemiologic study of medication use and Reye's syndrome.

1.notes: Title does not explicitly indicate case-control design, but abstract/methods clearly identify it

2.notes: Background: 'Reye's syndrome...etiology remains unclear...association reported with various antecedent viral illnesses...several medications investigated...aspirin receiving particular attention'

3.notes: Objectives: 'purpose of this study was to determine if consumption of medications during antecedent illness associated with RS differed from medication consumption of controls'

4.notes: Study design: clearly presented as matched case-control study

5.notes: Setting: Ohio. Study period: cases with onset December 1978 through March 1980. Single state surveillance-based case identification

6a.notes: Not applicable - cohort-specific item. Case-control details follow in 6b

6b.notes: Case definition: children with Reye's syndrome confirmed by CDC case definition; age-matched to controls; selection: 97 cases identified; Controls: 156 matched for age, race, sex, geographic location, time and type of antecedent illness

7.notes: Variables defined: primary exposure = medication use during pre-RS illness (aspirin vs acetaminophen vs other); outcome = Reye's syndrome diagnosis; confounders = fever, headache, sore throat

8.notes: Data collection: standardized questionnaire administered to parents/guardians; medication type, dosage, duration recorded; illness characteristics documented

9.notes: Efforts to address bias: matched design controls for age, race, sex, location, timing, illness type; logistic regression adjusted for clinical symptoms (fever, headache, sore throat)

10.notes: No sample size calculation described - study based on identified RS cases during surveillance period

11.notes: Quantitative variables: aspirin dosage categorized; maximum daily dosage compared; percentages calculated for medication use proportions

12a.notes: Statistical methods: logistic regression model to control for symptoms; odds ratio estimates; chi-square tests for categorical data; proportions and percentages

12b.notes: Stratified analysis: dosage-related analyses; assessment of relationship between dosage and stage of RS encephalopathy

12c.notes: No discussion of missing data handling

12d.notes: Matched analysis: conditional logistic regression used to account for matching; matched design explicitly acknowledged in statistical analysis

13a.notes: Participant flow: 97 RS cases identified; 156 controls matched; all cases and controls included in analysis

13b.notes: Surveillance-based case identification; matching criteria ensured control availability

14a.notes: Baseline characteristics: mean age and range of cases and controls; sex distribution; race; geographic and temporal matching factors

15.notes: Outcome data: aspirin use in 97% of cases vs 71% of controls; acetaminophen use in 16% of cases vs 33% of controls

16a.notes: Results with precision: significant difference in aspirin use between cases (94/97) and controls (110/156); adjusted for symptom presence; dosage comparisons reported

16b.notes: Dosage categories reported: aspirin maximum daily dosage comparisons; in 87% of cases receiving aspirin, dosage within recommended levels but higher than controls

16c.notes: Relative risk not applicable to case-control design; odds ratios not explicitly reported with confidence intervals

17.notes: Analysis by RS severity: relationship between dosage and stage of encephalopathy examined

18.notes: Key findings: 'only use of aspirin was significantly more common in RS cases than controls during pre-RS illness'

19.notes: No explicit discussion of study limitations

20.notes: Interpretation: results suggest possible association between aspirin use and RS; cautious language used ('possible association' not definitive causal claim)

21.notes: Limited generalizability discussion; Ohio-based study may limit applicability
